description: Book a meeting on Tuesday at 3pm
script: Can you check if Tuesday at 3pm is available?
Perfect, please go ahead and book it for Tuesday at 3pm
wer_note: Read each line exactly as written — WER is computed against these exact words.
notes: Baseline task. Single check + confirm booking.

description: Book a meeting for Monday at 10am for 5 people
script: Please book a meeting for Monday at 10am for 5 people
wer_note: Single-line task — read exactly as written. Key WER targets: Monday, 10am, 5 people.
notes: Direct booking with attendee count.

description: Check availability for Wednesday at 2pm
script: Is Wednesday at 2pm free for a one-hour slot?
Perfect, go ahead and block that time for me
wer_note: Read each line exactly as written. Key WER targets: Wednesday, 2pm.

description: Book a room for Friday morning
script: Please schedule a team sync for Friday at 9am
wer_note: Read exactly as written. Key WER targets: Friday, 9am.

description: Correct a time — fifteen not fifty
script: Please book a meeting at fifty past two on Thursday afternoon
Actually I meant fifteen past two on Thursday, not fifty past two
wer_note: Say both lines exactly as written. The key test: can the model hear 'fifteen' vs 'fifty' — both lines must be transcribed accurately for WER.
notes: Tests correction handling — similar-sounding number confusion.

description: Correct time from 4 to 4:30
script: Can you book me in for 4 o'clock on Monday please
Actually make it 4:30 on Monday, not 4 o'clock
wer_note: Say both lines exactly. The correction line must be transcribed correctly to show the model understood the update.

description: Correct attendee count — ninety vs nineteen
script: Please book a room on Tuesday at 11am for ninety people
I'm sorry, I meant nineteen people, not ninety — please correct that
wer_note: Critical WER pair: 'ninety' vs 'nineteen'. Say both lines exactly. Under noise both lines are phonetically challenging. Good WER test.
notes: ninety vs nineteen — classic mishearing under noise.

description: Correct the day — Thursday vs Wednesday
script: Can you schedule a one-hour meeting for next Thursday at noon
No wait, I said Wednesday not Thursday — please change the day to Wednesday
wer_note: Say both lines exactly. Key WER pair: Thursday vs Wednesday. Both are phonetically distinct but easy to mis-transcribe under room noise.

description: Spell out a contact name letter by letter
script: I need to book a meeting with a contact named Szymanski on Friday at 3pm
The spelling is S-Z-Y-M-A-N-S-K-I
Yes that's correct, please go ahead and confirm the booking
wer_note: Say all three lines exactly. The spelling line is pure transcription test — each letter must be captured. Confirm line tests short utterance accuracy after a complex prior exchange.
notes: Tests letter-by-letter spelling recovery — critical under noise.

description: Spell out an unusual room name
script: Please book room Vega for Wednesday at 2pm
The room name is V-E-G-A, like the star Vega
wer_note: Say both lines exactly. Letter-by-letter spelling after the room name provides a cross-check on transcription accuracy.

description: Update a contact name after spelling correction
script: Please update the booking name from Smith to Smythe
The new name is spelled S-M-Y-T-H-E
wer_note: Say both lines exactly. 'Smith' vs 'Smythe' is a near-homophone pair — good transcription stress test.

description: Spell a name with phonetic alphabet
script: The contact name is Przybyszewski
That's P-R-Z-Y-B-Y-S-Z-E-W-S-K-I
wer_note: Hardest spelling task. Say both lines exactly. First line is a full 6-syllable word the transcriber must handle. Second line is 13 individual letters — every single one counts toward WER.

description: Cancel Monday meeting and rebook for Tuesday
script: Please cancel my Monday 2pm meeting
Now book it for Tuesday at 2pm instead
wer_note: Say both lines exactly as written. Two-line task tests whether transcription accuracy holds across a multi-turn exchange.
notes: Requires two correct sequential tool calls.

description: Check, book, then cancel and rebook
script: Can you check if Thursday at 11am is free please
Great, go ahead and book that for 10 people
Actually cancel that and move the meeting to Friday at 11am instead
wer_note: Say all three lines exactly. Three-step task — WER is measured per line. 'Thursday' vs 'Friday' in lines 1 and 3 is the key transcription test.

description: Book two back-to-back meetings
script: Please book a standup on Monday at 9am
And also book a review on Monday at 10am for 5 people
wer_note: Say both lines exactly. Two times in one session — 9am vs 10am is the critical distinction.

description: Cancel all Wednesday meetings then book one
script: Please cancel the Wednesday 10am meeting
Also cancel the Wednesday 2pm meeting
Now book a single three-hour block on Wednesday starting at noon
wer_note: Say all three lines exactly. Two cancellations then one booking — WER measured against each line. 10am and 2pm in lines 1–2 must both be correctly transcribed.

description: Interrupt agent mid-response to change a detail
script: Please book a team meeting for next Tuesday at 3pm for 6 people
[INTERRUPT] Actually make it 4pm not 3pm please
wer_note: Read line 1 exactly, then press Next Line and immediately interrupt the agent mid-response with line 2 word-for-word. WER is measured on both lines — line 2 tests transcription accuracy during an interruption.
notes: Read line 1, then interrupt the agent's response with line 2 before it finishes. [INTERRUPT] lines are excluded from WER scoring.

description: Interrupt to change the attendee count
script: Please schedule a review session for Thursday at 2pm for 20 people
[INTERRUPT] Sorry, that should be only 8 people not 20
wer_note: Read line 1 exactly, then interrupt with line 2 word-for-word. Key WER numbers: 20 vs 8.

description: Interrupt to change the day
script: Please book the main conference room for Friday at 10am
[INTERRUPT] I meant Thursday not Friday, sorry about that
wer_note: Read line 1 exactly, then interrupt with line 2. Thursday vs Friday under mid-response noise is the key transcription challenge.

description: Multiple interruptions in one session
script: Can you check if Monday at 2pm works for a 10-person meeting
[INTERRUPT] Actually change that to 3pm please
[INTERRUPT] And make it only 5 people not 10
wer_note: Two consecutive interruptions. Each [INTERRUPT] line is said mid-agent-response. Numbers 2pm→3pm and 10→5 must both be transcribed correctly across the interruptions.

description: Trigger a long agent response, stay silent, then give a short follow-up
script: Can you tell me about all the available meeting rooms, their capacity, and which floors they're on
[WAIT 8 SECONDS]
Please book the largest available room for Friday at 2pm
wer_note: Say line 1 exactly, then stay completely silent during the 8-second wait while the agent speaks. Say line 3 exactly. Line 3 WER tests whether your voice is transcribed cleanly after the agent echo has settled.
notes: First line triggers a long response. Stay completely silent while agent talks. Echo from agent should NOT cause interruption. Second line tests if the conversation state survived.

description: Two exchanges where agent talks at length each time
script: What does my schedule look like for next week? Give me a full detailed rundown please
[WAIT 5 SECONDS]
Please cancel all of my meetings on Wednesday
[WAIT 3 SECONDS]
Now tell me what Wednesday looks like after those cancellations
wer_note: Stay silent during [WAIT] periods while the agent speaks. Say lines 1, 3, 5 exactly. Each spoken line follows a period of agent echo — this tests transcription accuracy under cumulative reverb.
notes: Three turns with long agent responses between each. Maximum echo exposure. The cancel call must have the correct day despite echo from prior responses.

description: Long silence after agent speaks, then a precise request
script: Can you explain how the booking system works in detail please
[WAIT 7 SECONDS]
Please book room B2 for Tuesday at 10am for 4 people
wer_note: Stay completely silent during the 7-second wait. Say line 3 exactly — it contains room name, day, time, and count. This tests clean transcription after reverb tail from a long agent response.
notes: The 5-second wait after agent finishes tests whether residual echo/reverb tail causes late false triggers. The precise booking request after the wait tests if the full conversation context is intact.

description: Agent says numbers, echo bounces, user says different numbers
script: Can you check if there's anything booked at 3pm on Thursday for 12 people
[WAIT 3 SECONDS]
No please change that to 5pm on Thursday for only 8 people
wer_note: Stay silent during the wait. Say lines 1 and 3 exactly. The agent's echo will repeat '3pm' and '12 people'. You then say '5pm' and '8 people' — transcription must capture YOUR numbers, not the echoed ones. This is the primary number-confusion echo test.
notes: The agent echoes back 3pm and 12 people. You then correct to 5pm and 8 people. Tests whether the model confuses echoed numbers with your correction.

description: Phonetically rich sentence with numbers and names
script: Please book a meeting for thirteen people on Thursday the fourteenth at a quarter past three in the afternoon
wer_note: Read this sentence EXACTLY as written — it is the sole WER ground truth. Contains: written-out numbers (thirteen, fourteenth, quarter past three), a day name, and time expressed in words rather than digits. Read clearly at normal pace.
notes: Purpose-built WER stress test. The sentence is long and phonetically rich. Under noise, numbers-as-words are harder to transcribe than digits. Compare Raw vs VF WER on this task.

description: Similar-sounding days and times in one sentence
script: Can you check if Tuesday the seventh and Thursday the ninth are both free at two thirty in the afternoon
wer_note: Read exactly as written. Contains two similar-sounding days (Tuesday/Thursday) and ordinal numbers (seventh/ninth) — classic mishearing pairs under noise. One sentence, one WER measurement.
notes: Tuesday vs Thursday and seventh vs ninth — two phonetically similar pairs in a single sentence. WER will spike under noise as the model confuses them.

description: Names, spelling, and a precise time in one sentence
script: Update the contact name from Kowalski to Kowalczyk and book them in for Wednesday at half past four
wer_note: Read exactly as written. Two Polish surnames (Kowalski vs Kowalczyk — differ by suffix only) plus a time expressed as 'half past four'. A perfect WER sentence: proper nouns plus natural language time.
notes: Kowalski vs Kowalczyk differ only at the suffix — transcription must get the full name correct. Also tests natural language time ('half past four') vs digit format.

description: Long precise sentence with maximum phonetic density
script: I need to cancel the Friday afternoon meeting at a quarter to five and rebook it for Monday morning at nine fifteen for sixteen people in the Vega room
wer_note: Hardest WER sentence in the suite. Read EXACTLY as written at normal pace. Contains: two days, two complex times (quarter to five, nine fifteen), a written-out attendee count (sixteen), and a room name (Vega). Every word counts toward WER. Run this task under all three audio modes and compare.
notes: Maximum phonetic density. Purpose-built to show the largest WER delta between Raw and VF modes. Use this as the headline benchmark sentence.

description: Impatient caller — books a time then immediately corrects it twice
script: Book me Tuesday at three
[INTERRUPT] No, make it four not three
[INTERRUPT] And for eight people — not whatever number you said
wer_note: Use clipped delivery — no please, no thank you, impatient pace. Line 1 is the WER ground truth. [INTERRUPT] lines are said mid-agent-response without waiting.
notes: Aggressive/impatient persona. Short clipped sentences, no politeness. Interrupt as early as possible mid-agent-response. Tests whether the model can track rapid corrections without getting confused.

description: Cancel, then change mind mid-response, then add another booking
script: Cancel my Monday ten am meeting
[INTERRUPT] Actually leave it, just move it to eleven
And also book Thursday at two for four people
wer_note: Read lines 1 and 3 exactly as written. Line 2 is an interruption mid-agent-response — say it before the agent finishes. Key WER: Monday/Thursday, ten/eleven/two, four people.
notes: Caller changes mind mid-cancellation then stacks a second request. Tests multi-task handling under impatient delivery with an interruption in the middle.

description: Rapid correction chain — time and attendee count both wrong
script: Schedule a meeting for next Friday at nine thirty for six people
[INTERRUPT] Nine fifteen not nine thirty
[INTERRUPT] And it is four people not six, please just listen
Can you confirm when you have done it
wer_note: Line 1 and line 4 are WER ground truths — read exactly as written. Lines 2 and 3 are interruptions delivered with visible frustration. Key numbers: nine thirty→nine fifteen, six→four.
notes: Two rapid corrections delivered with frustration. Tests whether the model correctly accumulates the final state (9:15, 4 people) after two overrides.

description: Name correction argument — Johnston with T vs Johnson without
script: Update the contact from Johnston to Johnson
[INTERRUPT] No I said Johnston with a T, J-O-H-N-S-T-O-N, keep it as Johnston
And book them in for Wednesday at three pm
wer_note: Line 1 and 3 are WER ground truths — read exactly as written. Line 2 is an interruption. Critical WER pair: Johnston vs Johnson (one letter difference). The spelling in line 2 must be captured correctly.
notes: Caller corrects the agent mid-response — the name is actually staying the same (Johnston), not changing to Johnson. Tests whether the model can handle a correction that reverts to the original. WER focuses on the near-homophone Johnston/Johnson.

description: Simple booking with continuous office background chatter
script: Hello, I need to book a meeting room for Monday at two pm please
Yes, go ahead and confirm the booking
wer_note: Read each line exactly as written at slightly raised volume (outdoor voice). Background: ask 2–3 colleagues to chat naturally at 2 metres distance throughout the whole task — this simulates street/ambient noise.
notes: SETUP: have 2–3 colleagues hold a normal conversation at 2m distance while you do this task. Speak at normal-to-raised volume as if outdoors. This simulates a roadside or street environment without needing to go outside.

description: Fifteen vs thirteen correction under ambient noise
script: Please book a room for fifteen people on Wednesday at eleven am
Actually I need to change that to thirteen people, not fifteen — same day same time
wer_note: Read both lines exactly at raised volume over background chatter. The critical pair is fifteen/thirteen — both 2-syllable number words that sound similar under noise. Background: colleagues chatting at 2 metres.
notes: SETUP: continuous background conversation at 2m. Fifteen vs thirteen is the primary WER stress test — both are easily confused under noise. Compare Raw vs VF WER specifically on these two numbers.

description: Spelling a Japanese name over background chatter
script: The contact name is Yamamoto, can you find their booking
That is Y-A-M-A-M-O-T-O, Yamamoto
Please book them in for Thursday at noon
wer_note: Read all three lines exactly at raised outdoor volume. The spelling line is the key WER challenge — each letter must be captured over background noise. SETUP: colleagues chatting at 2m throughout.
notes: SETUP: continuous background conversation at 2m. Repeated-syllable names (Yamamoto, Ma-ma-mo-to pattern) are particularly vulnerable to mis-transcription under noise. The spelling fallback in line 2 tests whether letter-by-letter recovery still works in a noisy environment.

description: Multi-step task over sustained loud background
script: Cancel the Friday four pm meeting please
And book a replacement for Monday at four pm for ten people
The name on the booking is Nakamura, that is N-A-K-A-M-U-R-A
wer_note: Read all three lines exactly at outdoor voice level. SETUP: have 2 colleagues increase chatter volume for lines 2 and 3 (simulating louder traffic burst). Key WER: Friday/Monday, four/ten, Nakamura spelling.
notes: SETUP: 2–3 colleagues chatting at 2m, louder from line 2 onward. Three-step task under noise — tests whether multi-turn accuracy holds when every response must be given at raised volume over a sustained noise floor.

description: Caller cut off by kids, then resumes booking request
script: I need to book a team meeting for—
[WAIT 3 SECONDS]
Sorry about that — book it for Tuesday at three pm for five people please
wer_note: Say line 1, then stop mid-sentence. During the 3-second wait have a colleague shout something briefly (simulating a child interrupting). Then say line 3 exactly — it is the WER ground truth. Key: Tuesday, three pm, five people.
notes: SETUP: a colleague briefly shouts something during the [WAIT] pause (one or two words, child-like). Simulates a caller being interrupted by a child. Tests whether the VAD gate stays closed during the background shout and re-opens cleanly for line 3.

description: Caller has to repeat availability check — background voice covered the first attempt
script: Can you check if Wednesday at two is available please
[WAIT 2 SECONDS]
Wednesday at two I said — is it available
Great, please go ahead and book it
wer_note: Say lines 1, 3, and 4 exactly. During the [WAIT] pause a colleague makes noise (simulates a child — say something short like 'Mum!' or 'Dad!'). Line 3 is a deliberate repeat — WER should be identical on lines 1 and 3 if transcription is consistent.
notes: SETUP: colleague shouts one short phrase during the [WAIT]. The repeat in line 3 (same words as line 1) lets you compare WER consistency across two utterances of the same phrase. If VF+Gate is working the repeat should score similar to the first attempt.

description: Spelling a name while being continuously distracted
script: Update the contact name to Nakamura please, that is N-A-K-A-M-U-R-A
[WAIT 2 SECONDS]
Did you get that? Nakamura — N-A-K-A-M-U-R-A
Good, and book them in for Friday at four pm
wer_note: Say lines 1, 3, and 4 exactly. During [WAIT] a colleague makes a child-like noise. Lines 1 and 3 are the same spelling — WER should match if transcription is stable. Line 4 is the closing booking. SETUP: colleague noise in [WAIT] gap.
notes: SETUP: colleague makes noise (child sounds) during the [WAIT]. Repeated spelling under distraction — the model must correctly handle a re-stated contact name update as a repeat, not two separate update_contact_name calls.

description: Multi-step task with two background noise interruptions
script: Please cancel the Tuesday nine am meeting
[WAIT 2 SECONDS]
And book it for Wednesday at nine am instead please
[WAIT 2 SECONDS]
How many people does the Vega room hold?
Okay great, book the Vega room for Wednesday nine am for six people then
wer_note: Read lines 1, 3, 5, and 6 exactly. [WAIT] gaps have colleague background noise. Lines involve two days (Tuesday/Wednesday), a room name (Vega), and attendee count (six). SETUP: loud brief noise from a colleague during each [WAIT] gap.
notes: SETUP: colleague makes a brief loud noise during each [WAIT] pause (simulates kids). Tests full multi-turn task integrity when two noise events punctuate the conversation. The final booking in line 6 requires the model to have retained day, time, room, and count across all the chaos.
